sections.heading: Won't my insurance pay for an ambulance or emergency medical transport?
sections.text: Due to the nature of modern health insurance, there's a chance that you could be responsible for a large portion of your emergency transportation bill. Even if health insurance covers part of your bill, there are many scenarios where they may not cover the total cost. The ambulance that picks you up may be out-of-network, the reason for your trip may not be deemed a medical necessity, or you might still have to meet your health insurance deductible.

sections.heading: What if the ambulance that picks me up is out-of-network?
sections.text: There are over 10,000 ambulance companies operating in the United States. Some companies are run by cities and states, others are run by local or national companies. Most insurance plans only cover in-network ambulance companies. Even if you're heading to an in-network hospital, the ambulance itself could be out-of-network and leave you with a balance bill. MASA offers coverage for ALL ambulance companies operating in the United States and other protection areas, including Canada, Mexico, the Bahamas, Bermuda, and the Caribbean (excluding Cuba).

sections.heading: What is medical necessity?
sections.text: In order for health insurance to accept an emergency medical transport claim, they first need to decide whether or not the ambulance trip was a medical necessity. Medical necessity is established when the patient's condition requires emergency transport and any other means of medical transportation would endanger the individual's health. This determination can be vague, and health insurance may deny claims where a member later finds out their symptoms didn't meet the insurer's requirements for an ambulance. MASA addresses members' medical transport claims even if health insurance denies them.

sections.heading: What is a deductible?
sections.text: A deductible is the amount of money a policyholder pays for health care services before insurance begins to pay. In the context of an ambulance ride, a member could end up paying a majority of the bill just to meet their deductible, even though they pay a monthly premium.

sections.heading: Which ambulance company can I use?
sections.text: MASA works with all emergency transportation providers. In the event of an emergency, a member should call 911 and get to the hospital. When the member receives the bill from the ambulance company, they send it to MASA. There are thousands of ambulance companies operating throughout the protection area, and MASA membership effectively covers all of them.

sections.heading: Should I pay the ambulance bill?
sections.text: MASA members should not pay their ambulance bill directly. Instead, send the bill to MASA with the member number clearly written on the front, either by email to ambulanceclaims@masaglobal.com, by fax to 817-681-2399, or by mail to MASA Member Services at 1301 International Parkway, Suite 300, Sunrise, FL 33323. Members can also log in to the member portal to submit a bill or check the status of an existing submission.

sections.heading: Do I call MASA at the time of an emergency?
sections.text: No. In the event of an emergency, call 911 and get to the hospital first. Only after receiving a bill from the ambulance company should the member send it to MASA. If the member is in stable condition and a doctor wants to transfer them to a different medical facility for specialized care, they should call MASA at that point to help facilitate transportation.

sections.heading: Does MASA provide protection if I get sick outside the US?
sections.text: For most plans, the basic protection area includes the United States, Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean (excluding Cuba), the Bahamas, and Bermuda. Members traveling outside these areas must submit their itinerary to MASA 10 days before the trip to adjust their global emergency medical transportation protection area. Members should check their plan documents to confirm whether they have international protection.

sections.heading: What should my trip itinerary submission include?
sections.text: When traveling outside the basic protection area, a member's itinerary submission should include: their name, member ID, additional travelers on the plan, the MASA plan number, date of departure and return, and destination. This can be emailed to travel@masaglobal.com or submitted by logging into the member portal.

sections.heading: How can I refer a friend?
sections.text: Members can call their MASA Sales Agent directly to make a referral, or have their friend call MASA directly at 1-800-643-9023.

sections.heading: How do I add my grandchild or great-grandchild to my MASA membership plan?
sections.text: For certain MASA membership plans, grandchildren and great-grandchildren may be covered. Members should refer to their plan document for details and submit the grandchild/great-grandchild addition form available on the MASA site.

sections.heading: What countries are included in MASA's Caribbean protection area?
sections.text: MASA's Caribbean protection area may include Anguilla, Antigua and Barbuda, Aruba, the Bahamas, Barbados, Bermuda, Bonaire, the British Virgin Islands, Cayman Islands, Cuba, Curacao, Dominica, the Dominican Republic, Grenada, Guadeloupe, Haiti, Jamaica, Martinique, Puerto Rico, Saba, Saint Barthelemy, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Saint Lucia, Saint Martin, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Sint Eustatius, Sint Maarten, Suriname, Trinidad and Tobago, Turks and Caicos, and the U.S. Virgin Islands. This defined country list is subject to change based on U.S. travel advisories or legal prohibitions that may impact MASA coverage eligibility.

sections.heading: When should I call MASA?
sections.text: Members should call MASA after receiving a bill from any emergency medical transportation service, or if hospitalized more than 100 miles from home and, while in stable condition, need to: go to a different hospital, have a family member or friend brought to their side, be returned home after the emergency, have a pet, vehicle, RV, or motorcycle returned home, or get children or grandchildren home.
